# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Define a cell culture medium. List the major groups of components that a complete animal cell culture medium must contain.**

**Model Answer:**

> A *cell culture medium* is a sterile, buffered aqueous solution that supplies the nutritional, osmotic, and physico-chemical requirements necessary to support the **in vitro** growth and maintenance of cells.

A complete animal cell culture medium contains:
1. **Inorganic salts** (NaCl, KCl, CaCl₂, NaHCO₃, etc.)
2. **Energy source** (glucose)
3. **Amino acids** (essential + non-essential)
4. **Vitamins** (B-complex)
5. **Glutamine** (energy and nitrogen donor)
6. **Serum** (e.g., FBS, 5–20 %)
7. **Buffer system** (bicarbonate + CO₂ or HEPES)
8. **pH indicator** (phenol red)

*(3 marks — definition + 2 marks for grouped listing.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**What is the difference between a defined and a complex (undefined) microbial culture medium? Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

> - A **defined (synthetic) medium** is one in which *every chemical component and its exact concentration are known*. Example: **Davis-Mingioli minimal medium** for *E. coli*.
>
> - A **complex (undefined) medium** contains natural biological extracts (yeast extract, peptone) whose exact composition is not fully characterized. Example: **Nutrient Broth** or **Luria-Bertani (LB) broth**.
>
> Defined media are essential for nutritional and metabolic studies; complex media support faster growth of fastidious organisms.

*(3 marks — clear distinction + correct example each.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

> **(a)** Discuss in detail the composition and role of **Murashige and Skoog (MS) medium** as a plant tissue culture medium. List its major macro-elements, micro-elements, and the role of plant growth regulators. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the concept of **auxin:cytokinin ratio** and how it determines morphogenesis in plant tissue culture. Support your answer with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

#### Part (a) — Composition of MS Medium

Murashige and Skoog medium, formulated in **1962**, is the most widely used plant tissue culture medium. It is a **defined, high-salt** formulation suited to a wide range of plant species.

**Major macro-elements** (concentration in mg/L):

| Salt | mg/L |
|---|---|
| KNO₃ | 1900 |
| NH₄NO₃ | 1650 |
| CaCl₂·2H₂O | 440 |
| MgSO₄·7H₂O | 370 |
| KH₂PO₄ | 170 |

**Micro-elements** include MnSO₄, ZnSO₄, H₃BO₃, KI, Na₂MoO₄, CuSO₄, and CoCl₂, along with **Na₂-EDTA + FeSO₄** as iron chelate.

**Organic supplements**: sucrose (30 g/L), myo-inositol (100 mg/L), thiamine·HCl (0.1 mg/L).

**Role of plant growth regulators:**
- **Auxins** (e.g., 2,4-D, NAA, IAA) → promote **callus formation, rhizogenesis**.
- **Cytokinins** (e.g., BAP, kinetin) → promote **shoot proliferation, cell division**.
- The **balance** between these two determines morphogenesis.
- Optional vitamins and amino acids further support growth.

**Physical parameters:** pH adjusted to **5.7 ± 0.1** before autoclaving at **121 °C for 15–20 min** at **15 psi**.

*(7 marks — 2 for macro-elements, 1 for micro-elements + iron, 2 for organics + PGRs, 2 for pH + sterilization.)*

---

#### Part (b) — Auxin:Cytokinin Ratio and Morphogenesis

Skook and Miller (1957) established that the **relative ratio of auxin to cytokinin** determines the developmental pathway of cultured plant tissues:

| Auxin:Cytokinin Ratio | Developmental Outcome |
|---|---|
| **High (e.g., 2,4-D 2 mg/L : BAP 0.2 mg/L)** | **Callus induction** — undifferentiated cell mass |
| **High auxin, very low/no cytokinin** | **Root formation (rhizogenesis)** — e.g., MS + IBA 0.5 mg/L for banana rooting |
| **Low auxin, high cytokinin (e.g., 2,4-D 0.1 mg/L : BAP 3 mg/L)** | **Shoot proliferation** — multiple shoots from axillary buds |
| **Balanced (e.g., 1:1)** | Maintenance of **callus in proliferation** without organogenesis |
| **High cytokinin alone, no auxin** | Direct **somatic embryogenesis** in some species |

**Examples:**
1. **Tobacco pith culture** — high auxin/cytokinin ratio → callus; high cytokinin → shoots; high auxin → roots.
2. **Banana micropropagation** — MS + 5 mg/L BAP for shoot multiplication; MS + IBA for rooting.
3. **Carrot somatic embryogenesis** — 2,4-D for callus; transfer to auxin-free medium → embryos.

The ratio governs the **gene expression programme** that shifts cells from proliferative callus toward either organ formation or embryogenic development.

*(7 marks — 3 marks for theory, 2 marks for ratio table, 2 marks for examples.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:**
> Many students write "high concentration of cytokinin causes root formation" — this is the **opposite** of the correct answer and is a **1–2 mark penalty**. Always specify **ratios**, not absolute concentrations.

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

> **(a)** Classify **animal cell culture media** with suitable examples. Discuss the role of serum in animal cell culture, including its advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Classify **microbial culture media** based on (i) composition, (ii) physical state, and (iii) function. Give at least one example for each functional type. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

#### Part (a) — Classification of Animal Cell Culture Media

Animal cell culture media are classified into **two main types**:

**1. Natural Media (Undefined / Biological Fluids):**
- Whole serum (FBS, FCS, horse serum, human AB serum)
- Plasma and tissue extracts (chick embryo extract)
- Amniotic fluid
- These provide growth factors, hormones, attachment factors, and nutrients in undefined proportions.

**2. Synthetic / Artificial Media (Defined):**
- **Balanced Salt Solutions (BSS):** PBS, HBSS, EBSS — for washing and short-term maintenance.
- **Basal media:** MEM, DMEM, RPMI-1640, Ham's F-12, Medium 199, McCoy's 5A, Leibovitz L-15.
- **Serum-supplemented media:** Basal medium + 5–20 % FBS (most common).
- **Serum-free media (SFM):** AIM-V, CHO-S-SFM, Hybridoma-SFM (used in clinical and regulatory applications).

**Role of Serum:**
- Provides **growth factors** (EGF, PDGF, FGF, IGF)
- Supplies **hormones** (insulin, hydrocortisone)
- Supplies **attachment factors** (fibronectin, laminin)
- Acts as a **buffer** and **detoxifying agent**
- Provides **binding proteins** (albumin, transferrin)

**Advantages:** Promotes robust growth, supports wide cell types, contains attachment factors.
**Limitations:** Lot-to-lot variability, risk of contamination (mycoplasma, viruses, prions), undefined composition, expensive, ethical concerns (animal source).

*(7 marks — 3 marks for classification with examples, 2 marks for role of serum, 2 marks for advantages/limitations.)*

---

#### Part (b) — Classification of Microbial Culture Media

**Based on Composition:**
1. **Defined / Synthetic media** — pure chemicals only, exact composition known. Example: Davis-Mingioli medium for *E. coli*.
2. **Complex / Undefined media** — contains natural extracts (yeast extract, peptone). Example: Nutrient Broth, LB Broth.

**Based on Physical State:**
1. **Liquid medium (Broth)** — no solidifying agent. Used for growth curves, fermentation.
2. **Solid medium** — contains 1.5–2.0 % agar. Used for colony isolation and enumeration.
3. **Semi-solid medium** — contains 0.2–0.5 % agar. Used for motility testing.
4. **Dehydrated medium** — powder form for industrial reconstitution.

**Based on Function:**

| Type | Purpose | Example |
|---|---|---|
| General-purpose | Grow a wide range of non-fastidious organisms | Nutrient Agar |
| Enriched | Support fastidious organisms | Blood Agar, Chocolate Agar |
| Selective | Inhibit unwanted organisms; allow target to grow | MacConkey Agar, Mannitol Salt Agar |
| Differential | Distinguish organisms by biochemical reaction | EMB Agar, Blood Agar |
| Enrichment broth | Selectively grow target organism from mixed sample | Selenite F broth |
| Transport | Maintain specimen without multiplication | Cary-Blair medium, Amies medium |
| Anaerobic | Support anaerobic bacteria | Thioglycollate broth, Robertson's cooked meat |
| Assay | Test antimicrobial susceptibility | Mueller-Hinton Agar |
| Enumeration | Count viable cells (CFU) | Plate Count Agar |

*(7 marks — 2 marks for each basis of classification with one example, 1 mark for the differentiation functional table.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mistakes:**
> 1. Students often confuse **enrichment** with **enriched** media. **Enriched** media are *solid* and contain extra nutrients (e.g., Blood Agar); **enrichment** media are *liquid broths* used to selectively increase the proportion of a target organism from a mixed sample (e.g., Selenite F broth).
> 2. **MacConkey Agar** is *both selective AND differential* — it is selective for Gram-negative bacteria (crystal violet inhibits Gram-positives) and differential for lactose fermenters (pink colonies). Most students mention only one role and lose 1 mark.
> 3. The correct term is **Sabouraud Dextrose Agar (SDA)**, not "Sabourad's agar" — spelling is checked.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2**

### Plant Cell Culture Media
- **MS medium (Murashige & Skoog, 1962)** is the universal standard.
- Five MS macro-elements: KNO₃ (1900), NH₄NO₃ (1650), CaCl₂ (440), MgSO₄ (370), KH₂PO₄ (170 mg/L).
- **Fe must be supplied as Fe-EDTA chelate** — free iron precipitates.
- **Sucrose** (30 g/L) is the carbon source; **myo-inositol** (100 mg/L) is a vitamin.
- **Auxin:Cytokinin ratio** is the master switch — high auxin → roots/callus; high cytokinin → shoots.
- **pH 5.7** before autoclaving; agar = **0.8 %** for plant media.
- Always filter-sterilize **thermolabile PGRs** (GA₃, 2,4-D stock) and add after autoclaving.

### Animal Cell Culture Media
- Two classes: **Natural** (serum, plasma) and **Synthetic / Defined** (MEM, DMEM, RPMI-1640, Ham's F-12).
- **DMEM** = high glucose; **RPMI-1640** = lymphocytes/hybridomas; **MEM** = minimum essential; **Ham's F-12** = CHO/serum-free.
- **5 % CO₂ + bicarbonate buffer** maintains physiological pH 7.2–7.4.
- **FBS (Fetal Bovine Serum)** is the most widely used supplement (5–20 %).
- **Glutamine** is an essential, unstable amino acid — add separately.
- **Phenol red** is the pH indicator.
- **Serum-free media** are mandatory for clinical/regulatory applications.

### Microbial Culture Media
- Classification by **composition**: defined (synthetic) vs complex (undefined).
- Classification by **physical state**: liquid, solid (1.5–2 % agar), semi-solid (0.2–0.5 %), dehydrated.
- Classification by **function** — 9 types to memorize: general, enriched, selective, differential, enrichment, transport, anaerobic, assay, enumeration.
- **MacConkey** = selective (Gram-negative) + differential (lactose fermentation, pink).
- **EMB** = differential (E. coli metallic green sheen).
- **Blood Agar** = enriched + differential (α, β, γ haemolysis patterns).
- **Mueller-Hinton** = standard for antibiotic susceptibility test (CLSI).
- **Sabouraud Dextrose Agar (SDA), pH 5.6** = fungal culture.
- **Thioglycollate broth** = anaerobic culture.
- **Cary-Blair / Amies** = transport media.

### Numericals to Practice
- Henderson-Hasselbalch: $\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a + \log \dfrac{[\text{HCO}_3^-]}{[\text{H}_2\text{CO}_3]}$.
- Exponential growth: $N = N_0 e^{\mu t}$.
- Doubling time: $t_d = \dfrac{0.693}{\mu}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Formulae (Memorize for KTU 2024 ESE):**
>
> $$\boxed{\; \text{pH} = 6.1 + \log \frac{[\text{HCO}_3^-]}{\alpha \cdot p_{\text{CO}_2}} \;} \quad \text{(Bicarbonate buffer)}$$
>
> $$\boxed{\; \mu = \frac{\ln(N / N_0)}{t} \quad ; \quad t_d = \frac{0.693}{\mu} \;} \quad \text{(Microbial growth)}$$
>
> $$\boxed{\; \text{Auxin:Cytokinin ratio} \to \text{Morphogenesis outcome} \;} \quad \text{(Plant tissue culture)}$$

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ingredients of Media
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT753 - CELL CULTURE TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Ingredients of Media

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of animal cell culture technology, **Ingredients of Media** refer to the complete chemical and biological formulation of defined and complex solutions that supply *in vitro* cultured cells with all the essential nutrients, ions, growth-promoting macromolecules, buffering agents, and physical-chemical regulators required to sustain their survival, proliferation, differentiation, and metabolic homeostasis outside their native physiological environment.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme (PEBBT753 - Cell Culture Techniques, Module 2), a cell culture medium is an isotonic, osmotically balanced, sterile aqueous solution composed of:

- **Inorganic salts** (maintaining ionic strength and membrane potential)
- **Carbohydrates** (chiefly D-glucose as the primary energy substrate)
- **Amino acids** (essential, non-essential, and conditionally essential)
- **Vitamins** (water-soluble B-complex and ascorbic acid)
- **Fatty acids and lipids** (membrane biogenesis and signaling)
- **Nucleic acid precursors** (purines, pyrimidines)
- **Hormones and growth factors** (mitogenic and morphogenic signals)
- **Buffering agents** (carbonate/bicarbonate, HEPES)
- **pH indicator dyes** (phenol red)
- **Antibiotics and antimycotics** (optional, for contamination control)
- **Serum or serum substitutes** (undefined or defined macromolecular supplement)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The classical distinction between **Natural Media** (biological fluids like plasma, embryo extract, lymph) and **Artificial/Synthetic Media** (chemically defined formulations such as DMEM, RPMI 1640, MEM, Ham's F-12) is a **frequently tested concept**. The students must also understand the difference between **Balanced Salt Solutions (BSS)** like PBS, HBSS, EBSS and complete *growth media* that additionally contain amino acids, vitamins, and proteins.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The "Cell Kitchen" Model

Imagine you are preparing a **complete balanced meal for a very picky and delicate guest** who must live inside a sealed, climate-controlled glass house for weeks. That "guest" is the cultured cell, and the "meal" is the culture medium.

| Real-World Analogy | Cell Culture Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Drinking water (H₂O) | Purified, endotoxin-free water (Type I, resistivity 18.2 MΩ·cm) |
| Table salt (NaCl) | Inorganic salts maintaining tonicity and ionic gradients |
| Sugar in tea (energy) | D-Glucose (main energy source) |
| Proteins (dal, paneer) | Amino acids, peptides, serum proteins |
| Multivitamin tablets | B-complex vitamins, ascorbic acid |
| Cooking oil (lipids) | Fatty acids, cholesterol, phospholipids |
| Spices & flavor (regulators) | Hormones, growth factors, cytokines |
| Refrigerator temperature (5 °C) | Buffered pH (~7.2–7.4) and 37 °C incubator temperature |
| Food preservative | Antibiotics (penicillin, streptomycin) |

Just as a malnourished child cannot grow, a cell deprived of *any single essential ingredient* will fail to proliferate, undergo apoptosis, or display aberrant phenotypes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric for KTU Examinations:** All cell culture media are formulated assuming an **operating temperature of 37 °C**, an **osmolarity of approximately 280–320 mOsm/kg**, and a **pH window of 7.2–7.4** maintained typically by a **5% CO₂ incubator** (using the bicarbonate buffer system) or by **HEPES** (outside CO₂ dependence).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Media Ingredients

The ingredients can be systematically classified into the following hierarchical functional categories:

### A. Inorganic Salts (Bulk Electrolytes)

These establish the **osmotic pressure**, **membrane potential (Nernst equilibrium)**, and serve as **cofactors for enzymes**. Approximately **9,000 mg/L** of total salts may be present in rich media like DMEM.

- **NaCl** (Sodium chloride): Predominant extracellular cation; maintains isotonicity
- **KCl** (Potassium chloride): Major intracellular cation; sets resting membrane potential
- **CaCl₂** (Calcium chloride): Cell adhesion, signaling (second messenger), bone formation
- **MgSO₄** (Magnesium sulfate): Cofactor for >300 enzymes (kinases, ATPases)
- **NaH₂PO₄ / Na₂HPO₄** (Phosphate salts): Buffering and ATP/nucleotide synthesis
- **NaHCO₃** (Sodium bicarbonate): Major physiological buffer (with CO₂)
- **Fe(NO₃)₃, CuSO₄, ZnSO₄, MnCl₂, (NH₄)₆Mo₇O₂₄** (Trace elements)

### B. Energy Source (Carbohydrate)

- **D-Glucose** is the primary carbon and energy source. Standard concentrations:
  - **Low-glucose media** (e.g., DMEM-LG): **1,000 mg/L** (5.5 mM) — preferred for primary cultures and to avoid metabolic stress
  - **High-glucose media** (e.g., DMEM-HG): **4,500 mg/L** (25 mM) — preferred for high-density tumor cell lines, hybridomas
- **Alternative:** Galactose, pyruvate, fructose (in some specialized media)

### C. Amino Acids

Cells cannot synthesize *essential amino acids* and must obtain them from the medium. The 20 standard α-amino acids are supplied in media, plus **L-glutamine**, which is a critical conditionally essential amino acid acting as a secondary energy source and nitrogen donor for purine/pyrimidine synthesis.

- **Essential amino acids (10):** L-Arginine, L-Cystine, L-Histidine, L-Isoleucine, L-Leucine, L-Lysine, L-Methionine, L-Phenylalanine, L-Threonine, L-Tyrosine, L-Tryptophan, L-Valine
- **Non-essential amino acids (8):** L-Alanine, L-Asparagine, L-Aspartate, L-Glutamate, Glycine, L-Proline, L-Serine, L-Glutamine
- **L-Glutamine** is special: it is **heat-labile** (degrades to ammonia and pyrrolidone carboxylic acid at 4 °C) — this is a classic KTU viva question.

### D. Vitamins

- **Water-soluble vitamins:** Thiamine (B₁), Riboflavin (B₂), Niacin/Niacinamide (B₃), Pantothenic acid (B₅), Pyridoxine (B₆), Folic acid (B₉), Cyanocobalamin (B₁₂), Biotin (B₇), Choline chloride, *myo*-Inositol
- **Function:** Cofactors for cellular metabolism (TCA cycle, electron transport chain, DNA synthesis)

### E. Hormones and Growth Factors

- **Insulin:** Stimulates glucose uptake via GLUT-4 translocation, lipogenesis, and cell proliferation
- **Hydrocortisone (cortisol):** Glucocorticoid; promotes cell attachment and proliferation
- **Epidermal Growth Factor (EGF):** 6 kDa polypeptide, binds EGFR → cell proliferation
- **Fibroblast Growth Factor (FGF / bFGF):** 16–18 kDa, angiogenic and mitogenic
- **Platelet-Derived Growth Factor (PDGF):** Wound healing, mesenchymal cell mitogen
- **Transferrin:** Iron-transport protein, prevents iron-induced oxidative damage

### F. Fatty Acids and Lipids

- Cholesterol, linoleic acid, oleic acid — precursors of membrane phospholipids
- Some cells (e.g., hybridomas) cannot synthesize cholesterol and require it exogenously

### G. Buffering Systems

| Buffer System | Components | Working pH | CO₂ Requirement |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bicarbonate (Earle's)** | NaHCO₃ + 5% CO₂ | 7.2–7.4 | Required |
| **HEPES** | HEPES (10–25 mM) | 7.2–7.4 | Independent |
| **Phosphate (PBS)** | NaH₂PO₄ / Na₂HPO₄ | 7.2–7.4 | Independent |

> [!NOTE]
> The Henderson-Hasselbalch relationship governs the bicarbonate buffer:
> $$\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a + \log \frac{[\text{HCO}_3^-]}{[\text{H}_2\text{CO}_3]}$$

### H. pH Indicator

- **Phenol red** at **0.0159 g/L (15.9 mg/L)** concentration: Yellow at pH < 6.8, Red at pH 7.4, Pink/Magenta at pH > 8.2

### I. Antibiotics and Antimycotics (Controversial Use)

- **Penicillin G** (100 U/mL): Inhibits bacterial cell wall synthesis
- **Streptomycin sulfate** (100 µg/mL): Inhibits bacterial protein synthesis (30S ribosomal subunit)
- **Amphotericin B / Nystatin**: Antifungal
- **WARNING:** Routine use is discouraged as it masks mycoplasma contamination and selects resistant strains.

### J. Serum (The "Undefined" Component)

- **Fetal Bovine Serum (FBS)** at 5–20% (typically **10% v/v**) is the most widely used supplement
- Provides: growth factors, hormones, attachment factors, lipids, minerals, pH buffering, protease inhibitors
- **Alternatives:** Animal-free defined supplements (e.g., KnockOut™ Serum Replacement, ExCyte™)

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula & Constants Sheet

| Parameter / Component | Standard Concentration | Function / Role | KTU High-Yield Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| NaCl | 6,000–6,800 mg/L | Osmotic balance | Major tonicity contributor |
| KCl | 400 mg/L | Membrane potential | Mimics intracellular K⁺ |
| CaCl₂·2H₂O | 200–265 mg/L | Adhesion, signaling | Excess causes precipitation |
| MgSO₄·7H₂O | 200 mg/L | Enzyme cofactor | Critical for ATP function |
| NaH₂PO₄ | 125 mg/L | Buffering, nucleotides | P-source for DNA/RNA |
| NaHCO₃ | 1,500–3,700 mg/L | Buffering | 1.5 g/L = 17.85 mM (low); 3.7 g/L = 44 mM (high) |
| D-Glucose | 1,000–4,500 mg/L | Energy | 1 g/L = 5.55 mM |
| L-Glutamine | 292 mg/L | N-donor, energy | 2 mM (heat labile) |
| Phenol red | 15.9 mg/L | pH indicator | 0.05 mM |
| HEPES (optional) | 10–25 mM | Non-CO₂ buffer | pKₐ = 7.31 at 37 °C |
| FBS | 5–20% (v/v) | Growth supplement | Heat-inactivated at 56 °C × 30 min |
| Penicillin G | 100 U/mL | Antibacterial | β-lactam, Gram⁺ |
| Streptomycin | 100 µg/mL | Antibacterial | Aminoglycoside, Gram⁻ |
| Operating pH | **7.2–7.4** | Physiological |  |
| Operating Osmolarity | **280–320 mOsm/kg** | Isotonic to plasma |  |
| Incubation Temperature | **37 °C** (mammalian) | |  |
| CO₂ tension | **5%** | Bicarbonate buffer |  |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering and Industrial Utility

- **Biopharmaceutical Industry:** CHO (Chinese Hamster Ovary) cells grown in proprietary serum-free media produce monoclonal antibodies (mAbs), erythropoietin (EPO), and clotting factors. Optimizing a single ingredient (e.g., replacing glutamine with GlutaMAX™) can boost antibody titers by **30–40%**.
- **Regenerative Medicine:** Stem cell culture media (e.g., mTeSR™) require precise, defined growth factor cocktails (FGF2, TGFβ) for pluripotency maintenance.
- **Cancer Research:** Glucose concentration and glutamine metabolism are central to the **Warburg effect** studies.
- **Vaccine Production:** Vero cells cultured in serum-free VP-SFM media produce inactivated polio and rabies vaccines.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Calculations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Calculation 1: Preparing 1 L of DMEM from a Powder Pack

A common KTU-style numerical involves calculating the **molarity of NaHCO₃** in a 1 L preparation.

**Given:** Powdered DMEM contains NaHCO₃ = **3.7 g per pack**. You dissolve the entire pack in **1 L** of deionized water.

**Step 1:** Identify molecular weight of NaHCO₃.

$$
M_{\text{NaHCO}_3} = 22.99 + 1.008 + 12.01 + (3 \times 16.00) = 84.01 \text{ g/mol}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply molarity formula.

$$
C = \frac{m}{M \times V}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute values.

$$
C = \frac{3.7 \text{ g}}{84.01 \text{ g/mol} \times 1 \text{ L}}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute final molarity.

$$
C = 0.04404 \text{ mol/L} \approx 44.04 \text{ mM}
$$

This justifies why DMEM-HG (high glucose + high bicarbonate) requires **5% CO₂** in the incubator to maintain pH 7.2–7.4.

---

## 3.2 Worked Calculation 2: Henderson-Hasselbalch for Medium pH

**Given:** A medium contains [HCO₃⁻] = **24 mM** and is equilibrated with CO₂ at **40 mmHg** (5% atmospheric).
At 37 °C, the CO₂ solubility constant **α = 0.03 mM/mmHg** and **pKₐ = 6.1**.

**Step 1:** Calculate dissolved CO₂ concentration.

$$
[\text{H}_2\text{CO}_3] = \alpha \times P_{\text{CO}_2} = 0.03 \times 40 = 1.2 \text{ mM}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the Henderson-Hasselbalch equation.

$$
\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a + \log \frac{[\text{HCO}_3^-]}{[\text{H}_2\text{CO}_3]}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute.

$$
\text{pH} = 6.1 + \log \frac{24}{1.2}
$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate logarithm.

$$
\text{pH} = 6.1 + \log 20 = 6.1 + 1.301 = 7.401
$$

**Conclusion:** The pH is within the physiological range of **7.2–7.4** — the medium is correctly buffered.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical implication:** If you forget to gas the medium with 5% CO₂ or transfer cells to the open bench for too long, dissolved CO₂ escapes, [H₂CO₃] drops, and the pH rises above 7.6 — turning phenol red **fuchsia/purple**, killing the cells within hours.

---

## 3.3 Worked Calculation 3: Percentage of FBS Stock

**Given:** The supplier label says FBS is supplied at **100% concentration**. You need to prepare 500 mL of complete medium containing **10% v/v** FBS.

**Step 1:** Volume of FBS required.

$$
V_{\text{FBS}} = 0.10 \times 500 \text{ mL} = 50 \text{ mL}
$$

**Step 2:** Volume of basal medium required.

$$
V_{\text{basal}} = 500 - 50 = 450 \text{ mL}
$$

**Step 3:** Add antibiotics (optional, 1% v/v stock of 100× pen-strep).

$$
V_{\text{antibiotic}} = 0.01 \times 500 = 5 \text{ mL}
$$

**Step 4:** Final basal volume adjusted to 495 mL (to keep total = 500 mL).

This is a standard KTU 3-mark computation.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: Choosing the Right Medium

| Cell Type | Recommended Medium | Key Feature Required | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| HeLa (human cervical carcinoma) | DMEM-HG (4.5 g/L glucose) | High glucose, pyruvate | High metabolic rate |
| Hybridoma (mAb production) | RPMI 1640 + 10% FBS | Glutamine, low glucose | Antibody secretion |
| Primary fibroblasts | DMEM-LG + 10% FBS + non-essential amino acids | Low glucose | Avoids oxidative stress |
| CHO cells (biopharma) | CD-CHO + GlutaMAX | Defined, serum-free | Regulatory compliance |
| Embryonic stem cells | mTeSR™ / KnockOut DMEM | bFGF, TGFβ | Pluripotency maintenance |
| Lymphocytes / PBMC | RPMI 1640 + 10% FBS | HEPES buffering | pH stability outside CO₂ |
| Hepatocytes | Williams' Medium E + ITS | Insulin-transferrin-selenium | Liver-specific metabolism |
| Neurons | Neurobasal + B27 supplement | Defined, serum-free | Prevents glial overgrowth |

## 3.5 Common Errors and Corrections

| Mistake | Consequence | Correction |
|---|---|---|
| Forgetting to add glutamine | Cell death after 48 h | Add 2 mM L-glutamine or use stable GlutaMAX |
| Heating medium at 56 °C | Destroys glutamine, vitamins | Warm to 37 °C only |
| Storing medium at 4 °C for >4 weeks | Glutamine degrades to NH₃ (toxic) | Use within 2–3 weeks or aliquot and freeze |
| Adding antibiotics routinely | Masks mycoplasma, selects resistance | Use only for primary cultures |
| pH drifts to pink/purple | CO₂ escape; cell death | Return to 5% CO₂ incubator |

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A: 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List the major categories of ingredients present in a typical animal cell culture medium. Mention the role of two buffering agents commonly used.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A typical animal cell culture medium contains the following major categories of ingredients:
1. **Inorganic salts** (NaCl, KCl, CaCl₂, MgSO₄, NaHCO₃, NaH₂PO₄) — maintain osmotic balance and ionic strength.
2. **Carbohydrates** (D-glucose) — primary energy source.
3. **Amino acids** (essential, non-essential, L-glutamine) — building blocks for proteins and nucleotides.
4. **Vitamins** (B-complex, ascorbic acid) — cofactors for metabolic enzymes.
5. **Hormones and growth factors** (insulin, EGF, FGF) — regulate proliferation and differentiation.
6. **Serum** (Fetal Bovine Serum) — undefined supplement providing growth factors and attachment proteins.
7. **Buffering agents and pH indicator** (NaHCO₃/CO₂ and HEPES; phenol red).

Two common buffering agents are:
- **Sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO₃):** Works with 5% CO₂ atmosphere; physiological buffer used in Earle's BSS.
- **HEPES (4-(2-hydroxyethyl)-1-piperazineethanesulfonic acid):** Synthetic zwitterionic buffer that maintains pH independent of CO₂ concentration; useful when cells are handled outside the incubator.

*(1 mark – categories; 1 mark – two examples; 1 mark – roles and buffering explanation.)*

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the role of L-glutamine in cell culture media. Why is it often added separately, just before use?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
L-glutamine is a **conditionally essential amino acid** that plays multiple crucial roles in cultured cells:
1. It serves as a **secondary energy source** via glutaminolysis, producing ATP, lactate, and CO₂.
2. It acts as a **nitrogen donor** for the biosynthesis of purines, pyrimidines, amino sugars, and glutathione.
3. It is a major substrate for the TCA cycle, replenishing intermediates (anaplerosis).

L-glutamine is **heat-labile** and spontaneously degrades in aqueous solution to **pyrrolidone carboxylic acid and ammonia** — the latter being cytotoxic to cells. This degradation is accelerated at temperatures above 4 °C and at alkaline pH. Therefore, it is often **added separately as a sterile 200 mM stock** just before use, or stable dipeptide substitutes such as **GlutaMAX™ (alanyl-glutamine)** are used. *(2 marks – role; 1 mark – reason for separate addition.)*

---

## PART B: 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - July 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, describe the composition of a typical **defined cell culture medium**. List any **ten (10) major ingredients** along with their concentrations and specific roles. (7 marks)

**(b)** Discuss in detail the **role of serum (FBS) in cell culture media**. Compare and contrast the use of serum-supplemented vs. serum-free media in modern biotechnology applications. (7 marks)

---

**Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

A defined cell culture medium is a **chemically characterized aqueous formulation** that supplies every nutrient required for cell survival, growth, and proliferation in the absence of biological fluids. A typical formulation (e.g., DMEM) contains the following ten major ingredients:

| # | Ingredient | Typical Concentration | Role |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **NaCl** | 6,400 mg/L | Major extracellular ion; osmotic balance |
| 2 | **NaHCO₃** | 3,700 mg/L | Physiological buffer (with 5% CO₂) |
| 3 | **D-Glucose** | 4,500 mg/L | Primary energy substrate via glycolysis |
| 4 | **L-Glutamine** | 584 mg/L (4 mM) | N-donor, energy, nucleotide synthesis |
| 5 | **CaCl₂·2H₂O** | 265 mg/L | Cell adhesion, intracellular signaling |
| 6 | **MgSO₄·7H₂O** | 200 mg/L | Cofactor for ATP-dependent enzymes |
| 7 | **NaH₂PO₄** | 125 mg/L | Phosphate source, buffering |
| 8 | **Phenol red** | 15.9 mg/L | Visual pH indicator |
| 9 | **L-Arginine** | 84 mg/L | Essential AA; urea cycle |
| 10 | **Insulin** (in supplemented media) | 5–10 µg/mL | Stimulates glucose uptake; mitogenic |
| 11 | **HEPES** (optional) | 25 mM | CO₂-independent buffering |
| 12 | **Penicillin/Streptomycin** (optional) | 100 U/100 µg per mL | Antibacterial prophylaxis |

*Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram for the hierarchical classification.*

*(1 mark – definition of defined medium; 4 marks – 10 ingredients with concentrations and roles; 2 marks – neat labeled flowchart/table.)*

---

**Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Role of FBS in cell culture media:**
- Provides **unidentified growth factors** (PDGF, FGF, EGF, IGF-1) that promote cell division.
- Supplies **hormones** (insulin, hydrocortisone, triiodothyronine, progesterone) that regulate metabolism and differentiation.
- Supplies **attachment and spreading factors** (fibronectin, vitronectin, laminin) enabling anchorage-dependent cells to adhere.
- Acts as a **pH buffer** and provides **proteins** (albumin) that bind and transport lipids, hormones, and trace metals.
- Supplies **lipids, fatty acids, and cholesterol** essential for membrane synthesis.
- Provides **protease inhibitors** that protect secreted products and surface receptors.
- Provides **trace elements** in bioavailable form (selenium, iron bound to transferrin).
- **Standard heat-inactivation** at 56 °C × 30 min destroys complement proteins and reduces mycoplasma risk.

**Serum-Supplemented vs. Serum-Free Media:**

| Parameter | Serum-Supplemented | Serum-Free |
|---|---|---|
| **Composition** | Undefined, batch-to-batch variable | Chemically defined, reproducible |
| **Cost** | Moderate to high (especially FBS) | Higher initial; lower downstream cost |
| **Risk of contaminants** | Prions, viruses, mycoplasma | Minimal (regulatory friendly) |
| **Downstream processing** | Difficult (protein interference) | Easier (purification of biologics) |
| **Regulatory acceptance** | Limited (FDA prefers defined) | Highly preferred for biopharma |
| **Growth support** | Excellent for most cell types | Requires cell-line-specific optimization |
| **Examples** | DMEM + 10% FBS | CD-CHO, mTeSR™, Hybridoma SFM |

**Modern Biotechnology Applications:**
- Monoclonal antibody production in CHO cells (serum-free).
- Vaccine manufacturing in Vero cells using VP-SFM (serum-free).
- Stem cell therapy and regenerative medicine (e.g., mTeSR™1, StemPro™).
- 3D organoid and tissue engineering cultures.
- **Xeno-free and chemically defined** media are now the industry standard for cell therapy products (e.g., CAR-T cell expansion).

*(2 marks – role of FBS; 2 marks – comparison table; 2 marks – modern applications; 1 mark – conclusion on industry trend.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **various categories of growth factors and hormones** used in cell culture media. Discuss their mechanism of action. (7 marks)

**(b)** Describe the **buffering systems** used in cell culture media. With suitable equations, explain the **Henderson-Hasselbalch principle** and its role in maintaining physiological pH. (7 marks)

---

**Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

**Growth Factors and Hormones in Cell Culture:**

**1. Growth Factors (Polypeptide Mitogens):**

- **Epidermal Growth Factor (EGF, 6 kDa):** Binds EGFR (ErbB1) → activates **RAS/MAPK and PI3K/AKT pathways** → cell proliferation, differentiation, wound healing. Used at 1–10 ng/mL.
- **Basic Fibroblast Growth Factor (bFGF/FGF-2, 18 kDa):** Binds FGFR → tyrosine kinase activation → **angiogenesis, neurogenesis**, stem cell pluripotency. Used at 4–10 ng/mL in ESC media.
- **Platelet-Derived Growth Factor (PDGF, 30 kDa):** Released from platelets; acts on mesenchymal cells (fibroblasts, smooth muscle) → wound healing, glial proliferation.
- **Insulin-like Growth Factor-1 (IGF-1):** Binds IGFR → cell survival, protein synthesis.
- **Vascular Endothelial Growth Factor (VEGF):** Endothelial cell mitogen; angiogenesis.
- **Transforming Growth Factor-β (TGF-β):** Dual role — inhibits epithelial proliferation but promotes fibroblast growth and ECM production.

**2. Hormones:**

- **Insulin (5.8 kDa):** Binds insulin receptor (RTK) → **GLUT-4 translocation** → glucose uptake; lipogenesis; mitogenic. Used at 5–10 µg/mL.
- **Hydrocortisone (cortisol):** Glucocorticoid; enhances cell attachment; anti-apoptotic in some lines; suppresses differentiation in myoblasts.
- **Triiodothyronine (T₃):** Thyroid hormone; promotes differentiation in certain cell types (e.g., chondrocytes).
- **Progesterone, Estradiol, Testosterone:** Used in specialized reproductive biology cultures.
- **Dexamethasone:** Synthetic glucocorticoid; used in osteoblast and adipocyte differentiation protocols.

**3. Mechanism of Action (Common):**
Most growth factors signal via **receptor tyrosine kinases (RTKs)** that dimerize, autophosphorylate, and trigger:
- **RAS → RAF → MEK → ERK (MAPK pathway)** — proliferation
- **PI3K → AKT → mTOR pathway** — survival and protein synthesis
- **PLCγ → IP₃/DAG → Ca²⁺ release** — short-term signaling

*(3 marks – growth factors; 2 marks – hormones; 2 marks – mechanism of action.)*

---

**Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

**Buffering Systems in Cell Culture Media:**

Cells in culture generate **lactic acid** (from glycolysis) and **CO₂** (from oxidative metabolism). Both acidify the medium, lowering pH and causing cell death. Therefore, **robust buffering** is essential to maintain pH 7.2–7.4.

**1. Bicarbonate Buffer (Earle's System):**
- Most physiological system.
- Equilibrium:
  $$\text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}_2\text{CO}_3 \rightleftharpoons \text{HCO}_3^- + \text{H}^+$$
- A 5% CO₂ incubator maintains the gaseous CO₂ tension at 40 mmHg.
- Concentration of NaHCO₃ is **1.5 g/L (low, for non-transformed cells)** or **3.7 g/L (high, for tumor cells in DMEM)**.
- Requires a controlled CO₂ atmosphere.

**2. HEPES Buffer:**
- **N-(2-hydroxyethyl)piperazine-N′-(2-ethanesulfonic acid)** — a zwitterionic organic buffer.
- pKₐ = 7.31 at 37 °C — ideal for biological pH range.
- Used at 10–25 mM.
- **CO₂-independent** — useful for cell handling on the bench, flow cytometry, microscopy.
- Disadvantage: more expensive; can be cytotoxic at very high concentrations.

**3. Phosphate Buffer (PBS):**
- NaH₂PO₄ / Na₂HPO₄ system with pKₐ2 = 7.21.
- Used in **washing solutions** (PBS) and as a component of media.
- Not suitable as the primary culture buffer due to poor CO₂ buffering capacity and precipitation with Ca²⁺.

**4. Phenol Red as pH Indicator:**
- pH < 6.8 → yellow (acidic, e.g., bacterial contamination)
- pH 7.2–7.4 → red (physiological, normal)
- pH > 8.0 → pink/magenta (alkaline, e.g., CO₂ escape)

**Henderson-Hasselbalch Equation:**

$$
\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a + \log_{10} \frac{[\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}
$$

Applied to bicarbonate system:

$$
\text{pH} = 6.1 + \log_{10} \frac{[\text{HCO}_3^-]}{[0.03 \times P_{\text{CO}_2}]}
$$

At 40 mmHg CO₂ and 24 mM HCO₃⁻, **pH = 7.4** (calculated in Section 3.2). If the CO₂ level drops to 20 mmHg, pH rises to ~7.7 (alkaline stress). The cell's Na⁺/H⁺ exchangers and Cl⁻/HCO₃⁻ exchangers work harder to maintain intracellular pH (~7.2), depleting ATP and causing apoptosis.

*(2 marks – buffering systems; 3 marks – Henderson-Hasselbalch derivation and application; 2 marks – practical implications.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT** confuse **Balanced Salt Solution (BSS)** with a complete **growth medium** — BSS lacks amino acids, vitamins, and proteins.
> 2. **Do NOT** state that L-glutamine is "stable" in solution — it is **heat-labile** and degrades to ammonia.
> 3. **Do NOT** say that **antibiotics should be added routinely** — current KTU 2024-aligned answers must mention the *risks of masking mycoplasma and selecting resistant strains*.
> 4. **Do NOT** forget to mention the **5% CO₂ requirement** when discussing bicarbonate buffering.
> 5. **Do NOT** miscalculate molarity — always write down the molecular weight first, then substitute into C = m/(M×V).
> 6. For 14-mark questions, **always include a labeled diagram, table, or flowchart** — answers without visual structure often lose 2–3 marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A cell culture medium is an **isotonic, buffered, nutrient-rich aqueous solution** providing salts, glucose, amino acids, vitamins, hormones, growth factors, and serum.
- **Salts** maintain osmotic balance; **glucose** provides energy; **L-glutamine** is a critical N-donor but **heat-labile**.
- The **bicarbonate–CO₂ system** is the most physiological buffer, requiring a **5% CO₂ incubator**; **HEPES** is CO₂-independent.
- **Phenol red** indicates pH: yellow (acidic) ↔ red (7.4) ↔ pink (alkaline).
- **FBS (Fetal Bovine Serum)** is the most common undefined supplement at **5–20% v/v**, providing growth factors, hormones, attachment factors, and lipids.
- **Defined/serum-free media** (e.g., mTeSR™, CD-CHO) are the future of biopharma due to regulatory and reproducibility advantages.
- **Antibiotics** (penicillin 100 U/mL + streptomycin 100 µg/mL) are **optional** and discouraged for routine use.
- **Standard conditions:** 37 °C, pH 7.2–7.4, 280–320 mOsm/kg, 5% CO₂, ≥95% humidity.
- **Common media:** DMEM, RPMI-1640, MEM, Ham's F-12, McCoy's 5A, Williams' Medium E, Leibovitz L-15.
- **pKₐ of HEPES = 7.31 at 37 °C** — memorize this number for viva questions.
- **Molecular weight of NaHCO₃ = 84.01 g/mol** — required for numerical problems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Physiochemical Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT753 - CELL CULTURE TECHNIQUES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Physiochemical Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Animal Cell Culture Technology**, **Physiochemical Properties** refer to the rigorously defined set of physical and chemical parameters of the culture medium and its immediate gaseous environment that collectively govern the survival, adhesion, proliferation, differentiation, and metabolic homeostasis of *in vitro* cultured cells. Under the KTU 2024 Scheme, these properties are classified into two broad categories:

1. **Medium-Associated Properties:** pH, Osmolarity, Ionic Strength, Viscosity, Surface Tension, Redox Potential, and Dissolved Oxygen tension.
2. **Incubator-Associated Properties:** Temperature, Humidity, and CO₂ concentration.

The standard reference values that define a "physiologically mimetic" *in vitro* environment are:

| Parameter | Optimum Value | Biological Justification |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| pH | **7.2 – 7.4** | Mimics mammalian blood plasma |
| Temperature | **37 °C** | Core body temperature of homoiotherms |
| Osmolarity | **280 – 320 mOsm/kg** | Isotonic to plasma |
| CO₂ | **5 % (v/v)** | Maintains bicarbonate buffer equilibrium |
| Humidity | **> 95 % RH** | Prevents evaporative medium loss |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A deviation of just **±0.2 pH units** or **±10 mOsm/kg** outside the optimal window can shift a culture from exponential growth to apoptotic crisis. This is a frequently tested 3-mark concept.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hotel Analogy"

Imagine a mammalian cell as a **luxury hotel guest** who is extremely finicky about the room conditions:

- **pH** is the *air conditioning setting* — too acidic (cold) and the guest gets sick; too alkaline (hot) and the guest dehydrates.
- **Osmolarity** is the *bathroom water pressure* — if the water inside the cell (tap) is not balanced with the water outside (pipe), the cell either swells and bursts (lysis) or shrivels like a raisin (crenation).
- **Temperature** is the *room thermostat* — set too low and metabolism stalls; too high and proteins denature (the "guest" faints from heat).
- **CO₂** is the *fresh air supply* — it works in tandem with the bicarbonate buffer to keep the pH steady, just like a building's HVAC system.

The **culture medium** itself is the *hotel*, and the **incubator** is the *building*. Our job as biotechnologists is to act as the *building manager*, ensuring every parameter stays within tight tolerances.

## 1.3 Why These Properties Matter in Practice

> [!NOTE]
> **Why do we care?** Every enzyme, every receptor, and every membrane transporter in a living cell has a *narrow operational window*. Cell culture is essentially the art of recreating the *in vivo* tissue microenvironment inside a glass or polystyrene vessel. Failure to control physiochemical properties is the **#1 cause of experimental irreproducibility** in cell biology labs worldwide.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of Each Physiochemical Property

### 2.1.1 Hydrogen Ion Concentration (pH)

- **Definition:** pH is the negative base-10 logarithm of hydrogen ion activity.
- **Standard equation:** pH = −log₁₀[H⁺]
- **Biological significance:** Governs enzyme kinetics, protein tertiary structure, nutrient ionization, and waste product (lactic acid) toxicity.
- **Buffer system in use:** The **Hank's Balanced Salt Solution (HBSS)** or **Eagle's Minimal Essential Medium (EMEM)** relies on the **NaHCO₃ / CO₂** bicarbonate buffer system, governed by the **Henderson–Hasselbalch equation**.
- **Why 5% CO₂?** At 37 °C, an atmospheric CO₂ concentration of 5% maintains the dissolved CO₂ at a partial pressure that balances the 1.2–3.7 g/L sodium bicarbonate in the medium, keeping pH locked at 7.4.

### 2.1.2 Osmolarity and Osmolality

- **Definition:** Osmolarity = the total concentration of osmotically active solute particles per litre of solution (mOsm/L). Osmolality is per kilogram of solvent (mOsm/kg).
- **Why it matters:** Cells in hypotonic media swell and lyse; in hypertonic media they shrink (crenation).
- **Primary contributors:** NaCl, KCl, glucose, amino acids, and the buffering salts.
- **Operational control:** Measured with a **vapor pressure osmometer** or **freezing-point depression osmometer**.

### 2.1.3 Temperature

- **Standard setpoint:** **37 °C ± 0.5 °C** for most mammalian cells.
- **Molecular basis:** Affects the kinetic energy of molecules, enzyme turnover number (k_cat), membrane fluidity, and gas solubility.
- **Rule of thumb:** A 10 °C rise roughly doubles reaction rates (Q₁₀ ≈ 2) — but prolonged elevation above 39 °C induces **heat shock protein (HSP)** expression and apoptosis.

### 2.1.4 Viscosity

- **Definition:** Resistance of a fluid to flow, measured in centipoise (cP) or Pascal-seconds (Pa·s).
- **Why it matters:** Affects mixing during pipetting, settling velocity of cells, shear stress in stirred bioreactors, and diffusion of nutrients.
- **Water at 37 °C ≈ 0.7 cP.** Media with serum are slightly more viscous (0.8–0.9 cP).

### 2.1.5 Dissolved Oxygen (pO₂) and Dissolved CO₂ (pCO₂)

- **In vivo reference:** Arterial pO₂ ≈ 95–100 mmHg; tissue pO₂ ≈ 20–40 mmHg.
- **Most cell lines are cultured at ambient O₂ (~21% O₂ = 160 mmHg pO₂),** which is actually *hyperoxic* compared to tissues. This is called **"normoxia in culture"** but represents a stress state.
- **"Physioxia"** = the more accurate, lower oxygen tension (~2–5% O₂) used for primary cultures and stem cells.

### 2.1.6 Surface Tension

- Measured in **dynes/cm** or **mN/m**.
- Affects bubble formation, cell attachment, and meniscus shape in wells.
- Pure water = 72 dynes/cm at 25 °C. Media ≈ 70–75 dynes/cm.

### 2.1.7 Redox Potential (Eh)

- **Definition:** Tendency of the medium to either donate or accept electrons.
- **Relevance:** Important for suspension cultures, hybridoma technology, and the reduced state required for certain stem cell media.
- Standard culture media have Eh ≈ **+100 to +200 mV** under ambient conditions.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Property | Governing Equation | Variables & Units | Notes for Board Exam |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | pH definition | $\mathrm{pH} = -\log_{10}\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]$ | [H⁺] in mol/L (M) | Pure water at 25 °C: pH = 7.0 |
| 2 | Henderson–Hasselbalch | $\mathrm{pH} = \mathrm{pK_{a}} + \log_{10}\dfrac{[\mathrm{A^{-}}]}{[\mathrm{HA}]}$ | pKₐ dimensionless; [A⁻], [HA] in M | pKₐ of H₂CO₃ ≈ 6.1 at 37 °C |
| 3 | Osmolarity (sum) | $\Pi = \sum_{i} c_{i} \cdot i$ | c in mol/L, i = van 't Hoff factor, Π in mOsm/L | Sum over all dissociating species |
| 4 | Osmotic pressure | $\Pi = i \cdot c \cdot R \cdot T$ | R = 0.0821 L·atm/(mol·K); T in K | van 't Hoff equation |
| 5 | Cell volume change | $\Delta V = V_{0}\!\left(\dfrac{\Pi_{\text{ext}}}{\Pi_{\text{int}}}-1\right)$ | V in µm³, Π in mOsm/kg | Valid for ideal semi-permeable membrane |
| 6 | Q₁₀ temperature coefficient | $Q_{10} = \left(\dfrac{k_{2}}{k_{1}}\right)^{\frac{10}{T_{2}-T_{1}}}$ | k = rate constant, T in °C | For most biochemical reactions Q₁₀ ≈ 2 |
| 7 | Viscosity (Newtonian) | $\tau = \mu \cdot \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | τ = shear stress (Pa); μ = dynamic viscosity (Pa·s) | Culture media behave as Newtonian fluids |
| 8 | Reynolds number | $\mathrm{Re} = \dfrac{\rho v L}{\mu}$ | ρ = density (kg/m³); v = velocity (m/s); L = characteristic length (m) | Re < 2000 = laminar; > 4000 = turbulent |
| 9 | Henry's Law (dissolved O₂) | $C = k_{H} \cdot P$ | C in mol/L, P in atm, k_H = Henry's constant | For O₂ at 37 °C: k_H ≈ 0.0013 mol/(L·atm) |
| 10 | Nernst equation (redox) | $E = E^{0} - \dfrac{RT}{nF}\ln Q$ | E in V, R = 8.314 J/(mol·K), F = 96485 C/mol | At 37 °C (310 K), RT/F ≈ 0.0267 V |

> [!NOTE]
> **Board Exam Tip:** Whenever a numerical is asked, examiners expect you to (a) state the equation, (b) substitute values **with units**, and (c) quote the final answer with correct significant figures. Skipping units costs ½ to 1 mark per step.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles of physiochemical control in cell culture directly inform **bioreactor design** (e.g., CHO cell bioreactors for monoclonal antibody production at 10,000 L scale), **organ-on-chip microfluidics**, **3D bioprinting**, and **tissue engineering scaffolds**. The same Q₁₀ kinetics, pH buffering, and mass-transfer equations govern the scale-up from a 96-well plate to a 2,000-L stainless-steel stirred-tank reactor.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Henderson–Hasselbalch Equation for Bicarbonate Buffer

The **bicarbonate buffer system** is the cornerstone of pH control in mammalian cell culture. We will derive it from first principles.

**Step 1 — Equilibrium Reaction**
The dissolved CO₂ in the medium hydrates to form carbonic acid, which dissociates into bicarbonate and a proton:

$$\mathrm{CO_{2(dissolved)} + H_{2}O \rightleftharpoons H_{2}CO_{3} \rightleftharpoons HCO_{3}^{-} + H^{+}}$$

**Step 2 — Equilibrium Constant Expression**
For the dissociation of carbonic acid, the apparent acid dissociation constant is Kₐ:

$$K_{a} = \dfrac{[\mathrm{H^{+}}][\mathrm{HCO_{3}^{-}}]}{[\mathrm{CO_{2}}]}$$

*Note:* We treat [CO₂] and [H₂CO₃] as a single combined pool called [CO₂] for simplicity, since CO₂ hydration is the rate-limiting step.

**Step 3 — Take the Logarithm of Both Sides**

$$\log_{10} K_{a} = \log_{10}[\mathrm{H^{+}}] + \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{[\mathrm{HCO_{3}^{-}}]}{[\mathrm{CO_{2}}]}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange and Introduce pH and pKₐ**

$$-\log_{10}[\mathrm{H^{+}}] = -\log_{10} K_{a} + \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{[\mathrm{HCO_{3}^{-}}]}{[\mathrm{CO_{2}}]}\right)$$

Substituting the definitions pH = −log[H⁺] and pKₐ = −log Kₐ:

$$\boxed{\;\mathrm{pH} = \mathrm{pK_{a}} + \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{[\mathrm{HCO_{3}^{-}}]}{[\mathrm{CO_{2}}]}\right)\;}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Verification at 37 °C**
The pKₐ of carbonic acid at 37 °C is **6.1** (note: this is the *apparent* pKₐ accounting for the slow hydration step). If [HCO₃⁻] = 24 mM and [CO₂] dissolved from 5% CO₂ at 37 °C ≈ 1.2 mM, then:

$$\mathrm{pH} = 6.1 + \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{24}{1.2}\right) = 6.1 + \log_{10}(20) = 6.1 + 1.301 = 7.40$$

This matches the physiological pH exactly. ✓

## 3.2 Derivation: Osmolarity of a Typical DMEM Formulation

**Problem:** A student prepares DMEM (Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium) by adding the following salts to 1 L of water:
- NaCl: 6.4 g (MW = 58.44 g/mol, dissociates into 2 ions)
- KCl: 0.4 g (MW = 74.55 g/mol, dissociates into 2 ions)
- NaHCO₃: 3.7 g (MW = 84.01 g/mol, dissociates into 2 ions)
- Glucose: 4.5 g (MW = 180.16 g/mol, does not dissociate, i = 1)

Calculate the total osmolarity contributed by these four components.

**Step 1 — Convert mass to moles**

$$n_{\mathrm{NaCl}} = \dfrac{6.4}{58.44} = 0.1095\ \mathrm{mol} = 109.5\ \mathrm{mmol}$$

$$n_{\mathrm{KCl}} = \dfrac{0.4}{74.55} = 0.00537\ \mathrm{mol} = 5.37\ \mathrm{mmol}$$

$$n_{\mathrm{NaHCO_{3}}} = \dfrac{3.7}{84.01} = 0.04404\ \mathrm{mol} = 44.04\ \mathrm{mmol}$$

$$n_{\mathrm{Glucose}} = \dfrac{4.5}{180.16} = 0.02498\ \mathrm{mol} = 24.98\ \mathrm{mmol}$$

**Step 2 — Multiply by van 't Hoff Factor (i)**

For NaCl (fully dissociated into Na⁺ + Cl⁻): i = 2 → 109.5 × 2 = 219.0 mOsm
For KCl: i = 2 → 5.37 × 2 = 10.74 mOsm
For NaHCO₃: i = 2 → 44.04 × 2 = 88.08 mOsm
For Glucose: i = 1 → 24.98 × 1 = 24.98 mOsm

**Step 3 — Sum All Contributions**

$$\Pi_{\text{total}} = 219.0 + 10.74 + 88.08 + 24.98 = 342.8\ \mathrm{mOsm/L}$$

**Step 4 — Compare to Physiological Range**
The target is 280–320 mOsm/kg. Our calculated value of 342.8 mOsm/L is **hypertonic** by ~7%. In practice, this small excess is compensated by the *excluded volume effect* of dissolved proteins and by the dilute solution approximation. The literature value for DMEM is **~334 mOsm/kg**, which matches our calculation to within 2.5% error — acceptable for a hand calculation.

## 3.3 Worked Example: Effect of Temperature Drop on Reaction Rate

**Problem:** A CHO cell culture exhibits a glucose consumption rate of **2.5 mM/hour** at 37 °C. If the incubator fails and temperature drops to 27 °C, what is the new consumption rate? Assume Q₁₀ = 2.0.

**Step 1 — Write the Q₁₀ Equation**

$$Q_{10} = \left(\dfrac{k_{2}}{k_{1}}\right)^{\frac{10}{T_{2}-T_{1}}}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for k₂ (rate at 27 °C)**

$$\dfrac{k_{2}}{k_{1}} = (Q_{10})^{(T_{2}-T_{1})/10} = (2.0)^{(27-37)/10} = (2.0)^{-1} = 0.5$$

**Step 3 — Compute k₂**

$$k_{2} = 0.5 \times 2.5 = 1.25\ \mathrm{mM/hour}$$

**Interpretation:** A 10 °C drop halves the metabolic rate, dramatically slowing nutrient consumption but also slowing waste removal and growth — leading to potential nutrient depletion artefacts and asynchronous cell cycle arrest.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Interrelationship of Physiochemical Parameters

The following Mermaid flowchart maps how each physiochemical property in cell culture interacts with the others. A change in one parameter propagates to others through the interconnected arrows.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Incubator Temperature 37C] --> B[Enzyme Kinetics]
    A --> C[Gas Solubility O2 and CO2]
    A --> D[Membrane Fluidity]
    B --> E[Metabolic Rate Glucose consumption]
    C --> F[Dissolved CO2 Concentration]
    F --> G[Bicarbonate Buffer Equilibrium]
    G --> H[Medium pH 7.2 to 7.4]
    H --> I[Cell Viability and Proliferation]
    E --> I
    D --> I
    J[Osmolarity 280 to 320 mOsm per kg] --> K[Cell Volume Regulation]
    K --> I
    L[Humidity above 95 percent RH] --> M[Evaporative Loss Prevention]
    M --> J
    M --> H
    N[Viscosity 0.8 cP] --> O[Shear Stress in Stirred Systems]
    O --> I
    P[Redox Potential Eh positive 100 to 200 mV] --> Q[Protein Folding and Disulfide Bonds]
    Q --> I
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The central convergence point is **Cell Viability and Proliferation (I)** — every parameter ultimately feeds into this single outcome. This is why the KTU syllabus treats physiochemical properties as a *system*, not as independent facts.

## 4.2 Block Diagram: pH Control Loop in a CO₂ Incubator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[CO2 Gas Cylinder 5 percent] --> B[Incubator Chamber]
    B --> C[Medium Containing NaHCO3 Buffer]
    C --> D[CO2 dissolves and hydrates to H2CO3]
    D --> E[Dissociates to HCO3- and H+]
    E --> F[pH Sensor or Phenol Red Indicator]
    F --> G{pH within 7.2 to 7.4?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Steady State Growth]
    G -- No --> I[Trigger Alarm and Adjust CO2 Flow]
    I --> B
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is a classic **negative-feedback control system**. The setpoint is pH 7.4, the sensor is the phenol red color change (yellow at pH < 6.8, purple at pH > 8.0), and the actuator is the CO₂ inflow valve. Modern incubators replace phenol red with **IR (infrared) CO₂ sensors** for ±0.1% accuracy.

## 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram: Hypotonic vs Hypertonic Stress

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Cell placed in medium] --> Check{Medium osmolarity vs cell 300 mOsm}
    Check -- Hypotonic below 250 mOsm --> Hypo[Water enters cell]
    Hypo --> Hypo2[Cell swells]
    Hypo2 --> Hypo3[Membrane rupture and cell lysis]
    Check -- Isotonic 280 to 320 --> Iso[Normal volume homeostasis]
    Iso --> Iso2[Healthy growth]
    Check -- Hypertonic above 350 --> Hyper[Water exits cell]
    Hyper --> Hyper2[Cell shrinks - crenation]
    Hyper2 --> Hyper3[Apoptosis or growth arrest]
```

---
